title: sort and reverse
instruction:
`sort` needs `<algorithm>`. Pass begin/end iterators and an optional comparator.

`sort(v.begin(), v.end())` — ascending (default)
`sort(v.begin(), v.end(), greater<int>())` — descending
`reverse(v.begin(), v.end())` — reverse in place
successMessage: sort ascending, descending with greater<int>(), and strings alphabetically. sort is O(n log n) — introsort under the hood (quicksort + heapsort hybrid). The most-used algorithm in C++.

title: find, find_if, count_if
instruction:
Searching algorithms:

- `find(begin, end, value)` — linear search, returns iterator
- `find_if(begin, end, predicate)` — find first matching predicate
- `count_if(begin, end, predicate)` — count matching elements
- Check result: `if (it != v.end())` to know if found
successMessage: find returns an iterator — dereference with * to get the value, subtract begin() for the index. Lambdas as predicates make find_if and count_if extremely flexible.

title: transform — apply a function to every element
instruction:
`transform` applies a function to each element and stores results:

```cpp
transform(src.begin(), src.end(), dest.begin(), func);
```

Can also take two input ranges and combine them.
successMessage: transform is map from functional programming — apply a function to every element. Element-wise addition of two vectors in one call. This is the building block of every numerical library.

title: accumulate and reduce
instruction:
`accumulate` from `<numeric>` folds a range to a single value:

```cpp
accumulate(begin, end, initial_value);            // sum
accumulate(begin, end, 1, multiplies<int>());    // product
accumulate(begin, end, "", [](s, val){ ... });   // string join
```
successMessage: accumulate is fold/reduce from functional programming. Sum, product, string join — all the same function with different starting values and operations. multiplies<int>() is a function object from <functional>.

title: any_of, all_of, none_of
instruction:
Boolean checks on ranges:

- `all_of(begin, end, pred)` — are ALL elements matching?
- `any_of(begin, end, pred)` — is AT LEAST ONE matching?
- `none_of(begin, end, pred)` — are NONE matching?
successMessage: all_of, any_of, none_of — boolean queries over ranges. 72 in failing[] is >= 60, so any_of returns true. These replace manual loops with readable one-liners.

title: Build a data pipeline
instruction:
No starter code.

Given employee records (struct Employee{string name; string dept; int salary;}):
- Create 8 employees in 3 departments
- Filter employees earning > 70000 (use remove_if or copy_if)
- Sort by salary descending
- Transform to a vector of strings `"Name: $salary"` using transform
- Print each
- Use accumulate to find total payroll
hint: Use copy_if to filter into a new vector. sort with lambda comparator. transform to strings.

codeChecks.message: Transform to string representations using transform.

successMessage: A data pipeline using STL algorithms — filter, sort, transform, reduce. This is how every reporting and analytics system works at its core. Chapter 17 done!
